nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and sheltered seaward reefs with mixed sand, rubble, and coral; occurring singly or in small groups. Reported from roughly 2–30 m.

decorPreferences: deep fine sand bed (5+ cm) for nocturnal burying
well-established live rock with abundant microfauna

diet.notes: In the wild, the Ornate Leopard Wrasse has been documented feeding on small invertebrates picked from the seabed, including foraminiferans (tiny shelled single-celled organisms), small crustaceans, and other hard-shelled prey. In captivity it tends to require a mature reef system with copious copepod and amphipod populations to graze on continuously; over time most specimens accept vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp and other small meaty foods, and some take high-quality marine pellets and flake. Frequent small feedings — commonly advised at 2–3 times per day — help meet the species' high metabolic rate, and a connected refugium tends to help keep microfauna replenished. When prepared foods are refused, small live shrimp may need to be offered during the acclimation period.

avoidWith: fish that compete for sand-bed territory
multiple males of the same species in standard-sized tanks

summary: The Ornate Leopard Wrasse is an eastern Indo-Pacific reef fish generally considered expert-only, owing to high shipping mortality, dependence on a mature, microfauna-rich reef, and a requirement for a deep fine sand bed.
careNotes: The Ornate Leopard Wrasse (Macropharyngodon ornatus), also widely sold under the trade name 'False Leopard Wrasse', is widely regarded as expert-only and is best suited to keepers with mature, microfauna-rich reef systems. The species inhabits lagoon and sheltered seaward reefs from Sri Lanka through Indonesia and western Australia to New Guinea, typically over mixed sand, rubble, and coral at depths of 2–30 m. Females are brown to greenish with pearly to metallic-green spots arrayed along the scale rows and reddish coloration anteriorly, with a dark spot on the lower-front body; terminal males develop a red anterior and darker posterior patterning with green-yellow spots trimmed in blue, and lack the female's black humeral spot. Initial shipping mortality has been reported as high, with new arrivals often stressed and prone to refusing prepared foods for days to weeks; slow, low-light acclimation in a quiet quarantine is generally advised. Long-term success is commonly tied to a well-established tank with copious copepod and amphipod populations (tiny crustaceans that the fish grazes on throughout the day), ideally backed by a connected refugium to keep that population replenished. A deep, fine sand bed of at least 5 cm — preferably more — is generally considered essential, as individuals sleep buried at night and dive into the sand when startled. A tight-fitting lid is strongly advised, as these fish have been known to launch from open tanks when spooked. The species tends to settle best when introduced before potentially aggressive tankmates such as tangs, angelfish, hawkfish, or damsels. Like others in the genus, M. ornatus is generally treated as a protogynous hermaphrodite — meaning most individuals begin life as females, with the dominant fish in a group transitioning to male. Multiple males in a standard-sized aquarium tend to fight, so most keepers maintain a harem of one male with several females, or introduce a group of three or more juveniles simultaneously. Copper sensitivity in this species is uncertain: some sources flag M. ornatus as sensitive to copper treatments, while others report the species tolerating low therapeutic copper levels during acclimation and single out the closely related M. choati as the notably copper-sensitive member of the genus. Given the conflicting reports, approaching any copper treatment cautiously and monitoring closely is reasonable. The common name 'False Leopard Wrasse' reflects this species' close visual resemblance to M. meleagris (the Leopard Wrasse); identification by collection locality and male coloration is generally more reliable than by trade name alone.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in the aquarium hobby literature.

primary.name: Quality Marine
primary.notes: Designated primary after the LiveAquaria product URL https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/ornate-leopard-wrasse returned HTTP 404 at access. Quality Marine supplied the complete husbandry table: 75 gal min (~284 L), 72–78 °F (22.2–25.6 °C), pH 8.1–8.4, sg 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, max ~5 in (~13 cm), expert-only, peaceful/shy, reef-safe, carnivore, deep sand bed required, sealed lid required, protogynous hermaphrodite, and an explicit copper-sensitivity caution.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked max length (13.0 cm TL), depth range (2–30 m), preferred temperature (25.2–29.2 °C, mean 28.6 °C — averaged with Quality Marine 22.2–25.6 °C to a final 23–27 °C aquarium range), Indo-Pacific distribution (Sri Lanka, western Australia, Indonesia, New Guinea), family Labridae / order Eupercaria, diet (small invertebrates from substratum, trophic level 3.5 ±0.4), and reproductive mode (oviparous, distinct pairing). FishBase records the common name 'False leopard'. Sex-change mode not stated explicitly; genus-wide protogyny is taken as default.

additional.name: ERC Reef Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 6 in / ~15 cm — averaged with Quality Marine and FishBase to 11–13 cm range), min tank (60 gal / ~227 L — averaged with Quality Marine 75 gal and biased upward to 300 L given jumping risk, deep-sand requirement, and need for harem swimming space), expert care level, peaceful temperament, reef-safe, and confirmed acceptance of PE Mysis, soft pellets, and LRS Nano blend in quarantine.

additional.name: LiveAquaria Species Spotlight: Leopard Wrasses of the Genus Macropharyngodon
additional.notes: Genus-wide confirmation of protogynous hermaphroditism, high shipping/acclimation stress, jumping/escape risk, recommended sand-bed depth (≥2 in / ~5 cm), and ordering of introduction relative to aggressive tankmates. Notes M. choati as the noted copper-sensitive Macropharyngodon and reports acclimating other genus members in low-salinity therapeutic copper — conflicts with Quality Marine's explicit copper-sensitivity flag for M. ornatus, so fish.copperSensitive is left null pending further sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in the aquarium hobby literature. The species is oviparous (egg-laying) with distinct pairing during spawning, and is a protogynous hermaphrodite — a reproductive pattern typical of the wrasse family Labridae.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: High shipping mortality; obligate dependence on copious copepod/amphipod microfauna from established refugium-supported reef systems; refuses prepared foods initially; needs deep fine sand for nocturnal burying. Not appropriate for new or undersized reef systems.
